turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Does all of it get into tank service?

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: Again, the record does not reveal that. It is possible that some of these rolls went to other divisions of Blaw-Knox. Now, some of these rolls were also sold to other steel mills in the area, like Bethelehem Steel, Inland Steel, for different and varied purposes. Mr. Thomas was working in the roll foundry for approx­ imately a year. The record revealed that this division was not engaged in the production of armaments. He worked as a chain- man "hooker" and he was involuntarily transferred to the tank turret line after a year. same answer you've given me each time, and --

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: Well, the record reveals that he was going to ask the congregation to make a decision but the record does not indicate whether the congregation made the decision. However, Mr. Thomas did reach a decision that the work he was performing in the tank turret line was against his religious belief.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is there any claim in this case that his beliefs were not bona fide religious beliefs?

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: Well, the Supreme Court of Indiana

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: Yes, yes, Mr. Justice, even though it's with but not adopted by the religion to which this person belongs is protected by the First Amendment. That's essentially our claim before this Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mrs. de la Torre?

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: Yes, Mr. Justice?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: To the extent that you are quoting from the record or saying that the record shows, I take it the final guide for us is what the opinion of the Supreme Court of Indiana says insofar as it speaks to the question, as to what the facts are?

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: No, the facts appear in the record in the decision of the Referee that was affirmed by the Review

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: That is possible; yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mrs. de la Torre, suppose that Mr. Thomas had reached the point where all work was against his religious convictions. Would you still be here?

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: We would have to look at that claim under the test that the courts have advanced, is this a reli­ gious belief and is it sincerely held? But that is not our case. He has not become a nonproductive member of society, he has just said, I will not work in the production of armaments, but I am willing, able, and available to return to the roll foundry or to work in any other type of work that does not violate my religious belief. The Court of Appeals of Indiana said that the facts were not in dispute in that with those facts Sherbert was con­ trolling. The Court of Appeals of Indiana held that the dis­ to Thomas imposed a burden on the exercise of his First Amend­ point is whether this is a religious belief and whether it is sincerely held by this individual. That, as I said, is the threshold inquiry. The answer to both questions in Thomas's case is, yes. Thomas' belief is religious in nature. He is a Jehovah's Witness. Nobody has questioned that thus far.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Does that faith also bar participating in national defense if our country were invaded? Does it bar -- is it total passive resistance?

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: From reading the cases that this Court fact he said, the Referee, oh, I can understand, I can see your difficulty, and I have the utmost respect for your religious principles. And I'm quoting from the transcript. Those are the words of the Referee.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And what if the Supreme Court of Indiana had said after the Referee said what you have just quoted him as saying, we find that the Referee committed reversible error in believing Thomas?

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: The Supreme Court of Indiana never said that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But what if it had?

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: Then they would have reversed the con­ clusion of the Referee and we would still be here because we think that the record supports the conclusion of the Referee. cient evidence.

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: The constitutional claim is a federal question.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is it a federal question on the narrow hypothetical Mr. Justice White has just posed, that the state court has reversed the referee on what could be his credibility findings? Now, let's assume, hupothetically, that reversing on credibility of witnesses unseen by the reviewing court would be an error of some kind. Is it a federal question error?

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: It would not be a federal question if we didn't have a free exercise claim involved. But this Court

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- if he quit for religious reasons?

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: Are you asking me if he did or if the Court can look --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You're saying we may look at the record and make our determination.

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: I believe this Court can. Again, we submit that Mr. Thomas' opposition to work­ ing with armaments is a belief protected by the First Amendment. The Court cannot --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Could the connection be so tenuous that the reviewing courts could reverse? Let me take a hypothetical between some of -- in the mean between some of the hypotheti- face that threshold question. And then, if the answer is yes on both questions, then we're going to have to go to the test as advanced in Sherbert, which is what the Supreme Court of Indiana fails to do.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you said before, this being a case involving turrets for tanks, tanks are reasonably used only in warfare. We don't have those difficult questions.

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: No, we don't have them in here. In looking at the sincerity, since the courts according to the doc­ trines of Fowler and United States v. Ballard cannot inquire into the validity of the belief, the courts can and do, most of central role in his life. He's a Jehovah's Witness, his hobbies with the tenets of the Jehovah's Witnesses who are opposed to war. Furthermore, Mr. Thomas was willing to take a 50 per­ cent cut in his income in order to live his life according to his religious belief. And that, we submit, shows that his belief was religious in nature and sincerely held. The analysis, then, in a free exercise case, is, is there a burden on the free exercise of religion? And if there is, is it justified by a compelling state interest? In this case we believe that there is an infringement on his First Amendment rights, the same infringement that this Court found in Sherbert vs. Verner. a compelling state interest. This Court has consistently held that it will not consider matters raised here for the first time which were never briefed or raised in the lower courts below. It will do so only when there is plain error, and we submit that is not in this case, that is not our case. There's a concession that there is no compelling state interest. And under those cir­ cumstances we respectfully request this Court that the infringe­ ment on Thomas's First Amendment rights not be tolerated.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mrs. de la Torre, in your briefs you have not cited the Braunfeld case.

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: Yes? Amendment rights, which was not the test in Braunfeld. Braun- the test in Sherbert, which this Court has consistently applied ever since Sherbert was decided.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I thought you would have attacked it in some way, anyway. One of the amicis did, but you didn't.

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: Yes. Americans United for Separation of Church and State.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It's a pretty small point, but where is the Blaw-Knox factory located?

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: It's in East Chicago, Indiana.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In East Chicago.

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: Finally, we would like to indicate

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: Yes, taxes from -- contributions --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It's not a state fund?

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: I believe it's both state funds and

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But what the employer pays depends on his roll foundry were engaged in the production of armaments. And Thomas did not file a Title VII. He filed for a --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, wouldn't he have been willing to take a job as clerk in an office?

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: Oh, yes, but there was no such job, apparently. The record reveals that he sought the transfer immediately upon being assigned to the tank turret line. And in seeking this transfer he realized that all of the other functions at Blaw-Knox were directly involved with the manufac­ ture of armaments.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But the employer must now pay him, in ef­ fect; pay him unemployment compensation.

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: The employer and the state. Not only to that.

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: Probably.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If the answer is important.

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: Mr. Daily. ORAL ARGUMENT BY WILLIAM E. DAILY

turns.name: WILLIAM E. DAILY
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The answer is that the contributions are made solely by the employers to the fund. There are no general tax funds, although I would suppose that the employer views it as a tax on the employer to create the fund in the first,place. Before I get to the meat of my argument, I have to case and the case can be decided without addressing that issue. But if you do want to address that issue, the Review Board should be given an opportunity to present facts if the case should be remanded.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, who litigates before the Review Board? The employer?

turns.name: WILLIAM E. DAILY
turns.text: The employer --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- and the employee.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. DAILY
turns.text: -- and the employee.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What sort of proceeding would the Review Board put in such evidence? existence of a compelling state interest.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What cases of ours would you say they would be guided by?

turns.name: WILLIAM E. DAILY
turns.text: I think perhaps they would have to go back -- if the decision were against us to the point where we were required to have a hearing on a compelling state interest, we would have to look to Sherbert, perhaps Wisconsin v. Yoder, and that line of cases.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How would Yoder help you there?

turns.name: WILLIAM E. DAILY
turns.text: I'm sorry; I stated the wrong case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How would the Yoder case --

turns.name: WILLIAM E. DAILY
turns.text: No. Yoder will not help in compelling but it's certainly been interpreted by subsequent courts and by some decisions from this Court more broadly than the deci­ sion itself warrants. And I'll get to that in a moment. The petitioner in this case relies heavily on Sherbert v. Verner; in fact, almost exclusively. Therefore, the Indiana Supreme Court looked to Sherbert and attempted to distinguish it. The court, the Indiana Supreme Court noted that the opinior in Sherbert specifically limits itself. The language says, "We do not by our decision today declare the existence of a constitutional right to unemployment benefits on the part of all persons" — "all persons whose religious convictions are the cause of their unemployment." Now, despite that clear language, the petitioner in this case says that all persons are entitled to unemployment benefits under Sherbert. language in Sherbert, could say, I'm entitled to workmen's compensation -- I'm entitled to unemployment compensation.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. DAILY
turns.text: And the petitioner did not deny that that person should — in fact, the petitioner's position is that if there is a sincere religious conviction, sincerely held by an individual, that person is entitled to unemployment benefits no matter what his conviction is, even if it is that I should not be required to work at all.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's not this case.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. DAILY
turns.text: No. Obviously not. Indiana Supreme Court clearly points out the state interest to point, are to promote the stability in employment, to protect the citizens from the hardships of unemployment, preserving the unemployment fund. Those state interests are in my opinion as compelling or more compelling than some of the state inter­ ests already upheld in this Court. And there I will refer you to Braunfeld v. Brown, Reynolds --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Daily, I don't think it's quite correct to say that there were no state interests involved in Sherbert. The opinion noted that there was a state interest in preventing spurious claims which would also be present here, and also a state interest in not making, complicating the work schedule that the employer has to worry about putting people on, when secular interests or secular purposes. As I read the opinion of the Indiana Supreme Court, that Court based its decision on three general and independent grounds. There are three different grounds for sustaining the Indiana Court's opinion. First of all, the Court found that the statutory good cause requirement has a valid secular pur­ pose and a valid secular effect. In other words, the Braunfeld v. Brown test. Under that test we have a valid secular purpose, a valid secular effect; it is therefore a constitutional stat­ ute . Secondly, the Indiana Supreme Court found that the grant of an exemption to Mr. Thomas would conflict with the Establishment Clause in the Constitution, as a second indepen­ there could be a potential conflict between the religion is, first of all, examine the legislative purpose; secondly, examine the degree of involvement between church and state. The current Indiana practice withstands these tests . Legislative purpose is a valid secular purpose, the degree of involvement is almost zero. The only reason that the Court had to examine the sincerity or the contents of Mr. Thomas's beliefs was because it had to distinguish Sherbert, or perhaps Wisconsin v. Yoder. Under the current procedure in Indiana there is really no reason beyond those cases for an examination or for Now I submit that there is no reasonable secular sonable purpose stated in the briefs. I've noted one amicus brief seems to think that a secular purpose would be avoidance of conflict with the religion clauses and that argument seems to me somewhat circular. You can point out as a secular purpose for the denial of the relief an avoidance of conflict with the Establishment Clause. In any event, there is no valid secular purpose for the establishment of exemption; the degree of involvement esca­ lates terrifically if you grant an exemption for religious purposes. Then it becomes necessary to examine Mr. Thomas or people in his position to determine whether or not he actually has a sincere belief. You have to examine whether or not that

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, a personal religious belief against doing anything relating to making the weapons of war, direct weapons of war?

turns.name: WILLIAM E. DAILY
turns.text: Directly involved in it; yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He backed that up by saying that making steel alone, as he had previously, was not a barrier, and we can only infer that he meant that some of that steel might go into other purposes, and that he was only raising the question and he was helping to make a tank, for which there was no use except in war.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. DAILY
turns.text: I think that's clear from the record, that his only objection was to being put on an assembly line Mr. Justice Rehnquist, it was specifically his membership and adherence to the beliefs of Jehovah's Witnesses that he relied on.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. DAILY
turns.text: I'm not sure that he relied on his Jehovah's Witness beliefs, but on his beliefs.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, is that true from the record, when there is another Jehovah's Witness who is not upset by this situation?

turns.name: WILLIAM E. DAILY
turns.text: That's the point I'm making. It was Mr. Thomas' beliefs and not necessarily Jehovah's Witness beliefs.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So it gets down to a matter of a personal crystal clear, open to no possible doubt. QUESTION.: Well, it's a factor, I suppose, but the fact that another Jehovah's Witness went the other way would indicate it isn't a compelling factor. I don't believe --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Didn't the Supreme Court of Indiana say the question was unclear as to why he quit?

turns.name: WILLIAM E. DAILY
turns.text: Examining the record in this case, the Supreme Court in Indiana could not find a basis for finding that this was a tenet.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That probably led to the 4-3 decision in the Indiana Supreme Court to some extent.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It was still 4-3.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. DAILY
turns.text: Well, the minority in the Indiana Supreme

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is there anything in this record that

turns.name: WILLIAM E. DAILY
turns.text: There are a number of -- well, it is clear from the record that Jehovah's Witnesses are willing to work on a tank in this factory. There are Jehovah's Witnesses working there.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Working on the tank itself?

turns.name: WILLIAM E. DAILY
turns.text: Yes. There is --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: On the tank itself?

turns.name: WILLIAM E. DAILY
turns.text: There is no other --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Or the turret for the tank?

turns.name: WILLIAM E. DAILY
turns.text: I'm sorry; the turret for the tank.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. DAILY
turns.text: Under that interpretation refusal to work on Saturday would not be good cause.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So that part of the Supreme Court decision is plainly inconsistent with this Court's holding in Sherbert, isn't that correct?

turns.name: WILLIAM E. DAILY
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now, what was the third reason? You didn't get to the third, but the second one is, you agreed that we'd have to modify Sherbert also to accept the second ground, so I'm interested in -- what was it? -- I forget what the third one was.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. DAILY
turns.text: The third one was the factual issues as to whether or not there is a religious tenet involved, whether I take it we can read -- this is a constitutional fact type

turns.name: WILLIAM E. DAILY
turns.text: Yes, it is. There are a number of --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, let me put the question to you directly. I gather we're as free to read the record in respect to this constitutional issue as was the Indiana Supreme Court, are we not?

turns.name: WILLIAM E. DAILY
turns.text: I understand that you incline towards giving credit to the interpretation by the state of its own --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, that isn't my question.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. DAILY
turns.text: And that you would --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: My question is whether we are as free as record, the evidence of record.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I thought they set up the test of the so- called undisputed facts, that if there had been a resolution of disputed fact by the state, you accept the resolution by the state.

turns.name: WILLIAM E. DAILY
turns.text: The problem on this factual record is, of course, that there was no participation by anybody opposed to Mr. Thomas so we have to examine what he said now as to the threshold question as to when you begin that examination. I'm not prepared to answer that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Daily, as I understand the Supreme Court of Appeals.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He didn't get the award; I understand that. But on the question of whether his belief was a sincere reli­ gious belief, they both agreed with him, didn't they? . MR. DAILY They agreed with him on that point, yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And this is what we are talking about now? MR. DAILY Yes .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And what do you say the Supreme Court of Indiana said about the sincere religious belief determination of the Court of Appeals? Didn't they say it was unclear?

turns.name: WILLIAM E. DAILY
turns.text: They said that on the record they could not sustain the finding below that he had a clear religious conviction. was realized in Walz v. Tax Commissioners. As Justice White the state should have some leeway in interpreting the religion clauses. In this case the state has adopted a neutral position. That position does not clearly establish a religion nor does it directly infringe upon religious freedoms. I am making the same plea that every attorney general probably does before you and ask that you recognize the federal system and adopt a hands-off approach in this particular situa­ tion. The Legislature of Indiana has assessed the public need, as required in Everson v. Board of Education. The statute is neutral, as required in that estate, and even though there is an there was there was a concession of no compelling state interest and we would like to address the attention of the Court to pages 13(a) and 19(a) of our petition for certiorari where the Court of Appeals clearly said that the task of applying Sherbert in this case is focused by the Board's concession in oral argument that'no compelling state interest exists in this case to justify an infringement. So it is very clear, there was a concession in oral argument. Second, the Board contends that in Sherbert there was a discriminatory effect in relation to Sunday worshipers that is not present in this case. The truth of the matter is that Sherbert was decided on First Amendment, not on the Fourteenth Amendment equal protection provision. Also, the interests now asserted by the State for the because that would indicate knowledge on this petitioner that have to admit that he could not accept that job and then allege that his free exercise rights had been violated, because he had knowledge. But in this case he didn't. The Referee concluded that it wasn't until he was transferred to the tank turret line that he realized the armament-producing nature of Blaw-Knox. So he would not put himself in a position of having a conflict with his religious beliefs. Also --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Could I ask you, suppose that his request to the Jehovah's Witnesses had been answered by the religious body saying, there's nothing contrary to our religion to work in the turret factory. And he said, well, that's maybe your view of it as a group, but my own personal religious beliefs

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And you would say the same thing, then, if a person went to his employer and said, I can't work in a turret factory, I'm going to quit, and the employer said, well, why? I have a religious belief. And his employer said, well, what religion are you a member of? And he says, none except mine. I'm a one-man, I have a one-man religion. And -- but it's religious. I think it's contrary to the laws of God to go to war or to even work on the implements of war. You would be here in the same capacity --

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: Then we would have to urge this Court urge protection of his First Amendment rights, but in this case that backing is persuasive as to the issue of sincerity and is very crucial.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: but your response to Mr. Justice White was that eliminating that you would still be here.

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: Yes; yes, Mr. Justice.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What if he said, I'm an atheist but I'm opposed to all wars?

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: Then I would have to urge this Court to adopt the definition of religion in Seeger and Welsh.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But these fallback positions do not alter the fact that you have an organized established religion declaring the belief on which he relies. their own decision. Witnesses refuse to work in the turret factory.

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And at least some of them thought it was wholly consistent with the tenets of the sect, or of the reli­ gion .

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: They did not view working there as unscriptural, while Mr. Thomas did.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But does that necessarily follow? Suppos­ ing you had a Catholic nurse employed in a hospital and was transferred into the abortion ward, and she said, well, it's against my religion to work in abortions and then they defend

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: Well, we are submitting that the pres­ tected expression of religious belief.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You agree that the group as a whole do not make this a tenet, that they don't have a rule against working in turret factories. They leave it up to each individual to study the Scriptures and come to his own conclusions as to what his religious belief should be.

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: Yes, Mr. Justice.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So the fellow who works in the turret fac­ tory is acting just as consistently with the Jehovah's Witnesses tenets as the fellow who doesn't.

turns.name: BIANCHI
turns.text: His interpretation; yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes. 43 CERTIFICATE North American Reporting hereby certifies that the attached pages represent an accurate transcript of electronic sound recording in the oral argument before the Supreme Court of the United States in the matter of:
